pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: When questioned extensively, the patient's partner confirmed that Ms. EL had recently started taking a new, over-the-counter herbal supplement marketed for "stress relief." The supplement contained high concentrations of St. John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum), a potent inducer of hepatic CYP450 enzymes. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a partial deficiency of the enzyme hydroxymethylbilane synthase (HMBS), the third enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. Normally, this deficiency is asymptomatic. However, when exposed to triggering agents (such as certain barbiturates, sulfonamides, metabolic stress, or potent CYP450 inducers like St. John’s Wort), the demand for heme in the liver increases drastically. The limited HMBS capacity is overwhelmed, leading to the rapid accumulation of the neurotoxic heme precursors: delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and Porphobilinogen (PBG). The clinical triad is characterized by: 1. Abdominal pain (due to autonomic neuropathy of the visceral nerves). 2. Psychiatric/CNS symptoms (due to neurotoxicity of ALA/PBG). 3. Peripheral Neuropathy (motor greater than sensory, often mimicking GBS). The characteristic dark urine is due to the non-enzymatic oxidation of accumulated PBG to colored porphobilins upon exposure to light and air. The treatment involves immediate cessation of all triggering agents and high-dose IV Hematin to suppress ALA synthase activity, thereby halting the production of neurotoxic precursors.
